sections.markdown:
# The Sequence API

A **sequence** in Common Lisp is either a list or a vector (including strings). The sequence functions work on **either**.

The everyday sequence API:

* `(length s)` — element count.
* `(elt s i)` — element at index i (0-based). Works on both lists and vectors, but is O(n) on lists.
* `(subseq s start &optional end)` — sub-sequence.
* `(reverse s)` — fresh reversed copy.
* `(map result-type fn s &rest more)` — like MAPCAR but the result type is your choice (`'list`, `'string`, `'vector`, `nil`).
* `(reduce fn s &key initial-value from-end)` — fold.
* `(find item s &key test)`, `(position item s &key test)`, `(count item s &key test)`.
* `(remove item s &key test)` — fresh copy without matches.
* `(every pred s)`, `(some pred s)`.
* `(sort s pred)` — DESTRUCTIVE; pass a copy if you want to keep the original.

Using `:test #'equal` is common when items are strings or lists.

sections.explain: LENGTH works uniformly on lists, vectors, and strings.
ELT is the generic indexed accessor.
Same call shape works on a vector.
MAP returns the requested type — here a list, from a vector input.
Build a string by mapping over a string.
FIND returns the matching element or NIL.
POSITION returns the index of the first match.
SORT is destructive; pass a COPY-SEQ if the original matters.

sections.prompt: Define COUNT-CHAR that returns how many times character C occurs in string S.
Define SORTED-COPY that returns a sorted ascending copy of a sequence of numbers, without modifying the input.
Define REMOVE-NEGATIVES that returns a fresh sequence (same kind as input) with negative numbers removed.
